perturbation.original_text: (7) that in the event any proceeding to enforce this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement or decide any matter relating to it Recipient becomes necessary, the Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland pertaining thereto and the Recipient agrees to submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Canton of Geneva, Switzerland;
perturbation.changed_text: (7) that in the event any proceeding to enforce this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement or decide any matter relating to it Recipient becomes necessary, the Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland pertaining thereto;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause where the Recipient agrees to submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Canton of Geneva, Switzerland might create issues related to the enforcement of the contract. This could violate Swiss Civil Procedure Code.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 7 of the Swiss Civil Procedure Code defines the jurisdiction of courts based on the defendant's domicile or residence. By removing the clause where the recipient agrees to submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the Canton of Geneva, it is not clear if the Swiss courts have jurisdiction on the defendant depending on their residence. Therefore, it contradicts the law.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a clause where the Recipient agreed to submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Canton of Geneva, Switzerland. This clause has been removed in the changed text. The Law Explanation states that Article 7 of the Swiss Civil Procedure Code defines the jurisdiction of courts based on the defendant's domicile or residence. The Law Explanation further suggests that by removing the clause, it is unclear if the Swiss courts have jurisdiction on the defendant depending on their residence, contradicting the law. The omission of the jurisdiction clause, as explained, creates a situation where jurisdiction is not clearly defined, potentially hindering enforcement and contradicting the intent of Swiss civil procedure regarding jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: that the Recipient is aware of and has reviewed the ICoCA’s Conflict of Interest and Information Security policies and that Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information to any person within the Association (i.e., the Directors, Officers, or Staff Members) if the disclosure of such information would violate the Association’s Conflict of Interest or Information Security policies
perturbation.changed_text: that the Recipient is aware of the ICoCA’s Conflict of Interest policies and that Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information to any person within the Association (i.e., the Directors, Officers, or Staff Members) if the disclosure of such information would violate the Association’s Conflict of Interest policies
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'Information Security policies' in this clause, the Recipient is not explicitly bound to adhere to the information security policies of the Association. This creates a legal risk because the clause doesn't mention the security policies, which can violate the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) which requires organizations to maintain adequate security measures when processing personal data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP), Article 7
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 7 of the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) requires that personal data must be protected against unauthorized processing by appropriate technical and organizational measures. By omitting the reference to the ICoCA's Information Security policies, the contract provides no assurance that appropriate security measures are in place, potentially violating Article 7 of the FADP.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fedlex DE: Vielen Dank, dass Sie sie die Website des Bundesrechts aufgerufen haben; sie ist nur mit einem Javascript-fähigen Browser verfügbar. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. FR: Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du droit fédéral; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent. IT: Grazie per aver scelto il sito web del diritto federale; questo è disponibile soltanto con un browser che supporta JavaScript. Per navigare in modo ottimale in questo sito, vi consigliamo di utilizzare un browser più recente. RM: Grazia fitg che Vus visitais la pagina d’internet dal dretg federal; ella è disponibla mo cun in navigatur che sustegna javascript. Per pudair far diever da questa pagina d’internet en moda optimala As recumandain nus d’utilisar in navigatur actual. EN: Thank you for visiting the federal law website; it is only fully accessible if you are using a JavaScript-capable browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the phrase 'Information Security policies.' The Perturbation Explanation states that this creates a legal risk because the clause doesn't mention the security policies, potentially violating the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP). The Law Explanation says that Article 7 of the FADP requires that personal data must be protected against unauthorized processing by appropriate technical and organizational measures. By omitting the reference to ICoCA's Information Security policies, the contract provides no assurance that appropriate security measures are in place, potentially violating Article 7 of the FADP. The scraped law snippet 1 is empty, and scraped law snippet 2 discusses javascript capability, but it doesn't directly relate to the contradiction. Nevertheless, based on the perturbation and law explanations, a contradiction exists because the omission weakens the assurance of data protection measures required by the FADP.

perturbation.changed_text: Signed and agreed to this ___ day of ______________, 2015 __ ________________________________  Recipient for the Association
perturbation.explanation: Removing the space for the recipient to fill in their typed name and position could create issues related to the enforceability of the contract, because there is no mention of their position. This could violate Swiss Code of Obligations regarding contracts which requires clear identification of parties involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1 of the Swiss Code of Obligations states that a contract is formed upon mutual consent of the parties. Implicit in this requirement is the need for clarity regarding the parties involved. By removing the position field, the contract becomes less clear and it becomes ambiguous as to the role and responsibility of the recipient, which contradicts the principle of mutual consent and clear identification.

perturbation.original_text: that in the event Recipient violates any part of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement, the Association may pursue legal and/or equitable remedies from Recipient
perturbation.changed_text: that in the event Recipient violates any part of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement, the Association may pursue legal remedies from Recipient
perturbation.explanation: By removing the term 'equitable remedies', the agreement limits the Association's options for recourse against the Recipient. This introduces a legal contradiction because the Swiss Code of Obligations recognizes both legal and equitable remedies for breach of contract. Limiting the available remedies may prevent the Association from obtaining full compensation for damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 97 of the Swiss Code of Obligations states that a debtor who fails to perform an obligation is liable for damages resulting from the non-performance. In Swiss law, damages can be awarded to provide the creditor with the appropriate relief, whether legal or equitable. Removing equitable remedies may limit the creditor's ability to be fully compensated, thus violating the principle outlined in Article 97 of the CO.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Association to pursue 'legal and/or equitable remedies,' while the changed text limits this to 'legal remedies.' The Law Explanation states that Article 97 of the Swiss Code of Obligations aims to fully compensate the creditor, 'whether legal or equitable.' By removing 'equitable remedies,' the changed text potentially limits the Association's ability to be fully compensated for damages, thus contradicting the intent of Article 97, as explained.
